coredata.dc:title: A “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for hydroquinone detection based on BSA doped carbon dots (BSA@CDs) from crawfish shells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Yuliang
Zhang, Fan
Zhao, Yajie
Chen, Jiannan
Yao, Weirong
Qian, He
coredata.dc:description: By using crawfish shells as the precursor and hydrothermal synthesis, Bovine serum albumin doped carbon dots (BSA@CDs) were prepared without excessive chemical reagents. The relationship between the fluorescence properties of different BSA@CDs and BSA amount was investigated by variouscharacterization techniques. When the amount of BSA added was 30 %, the prepared BSA@CDs’ quantum yield (QY) reached 25.01 %, which was the highest. Inner Filter Effect (IFE) suggested that Cr (VI) can selectively quench the fluorescence of BSA@CDs. Cr (VI) can be reduced to Cr (III) by Hydroquinone (HQ), thus recovering the fluorescence. Accordingly, using BSA@CDs as a probe, a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor applied in HQ determination was constructed. The linear range was 10–200 µmol/L and limit of detection (LOD) was 0.18 µmol/L. Further, it has been employed to the determination of HQ in both crawfish tail meat and aquaculture water with good performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crawfish shells
Carbon dots
Bovine serum albumin
Hydroquinone detection
“Turn-on” fluorescence sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported in this article was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Changzhou (Project No. CE20232005), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province, China.

The work reported in this article was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Changzhou (Project No. CE20232005 ), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of BSA@CDs (The inset is HRTEM image).
(A) XRD pattern, (B) Raman spectrum, (C) FT-IR spectrum, (D) Nitrogen and sulfur content of BSA@CDs.
(A) Full-scan XPS spectra (high-resolution) (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum, (D) O1s spectrum and (E) S2p spectrum of BSA@CDs.

(A) Photos of BSA@CDs in supernatant, (B) Supernatant under UV irradiation, (C) Supernatant diluted to 100 times, (D) Supernatant diluted to 100 times under UV irradiation; (E) Fluorescence emission spectra and (F) UV–visible absorption spectra of BSA@CDs; (G) Fluorescence lifetime of BSA@CDs and BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ).
Effects of (A) BSA@CDs concentration, (B) pH value and (C) Reaction time on FI of BSA@CDs/Cr (VI).

(A) BSA@CDs’ fluorescence response to diverse metal ions. (B) BSA@CDs’ Selectivity to Cr (VI) with other ions added. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA@CDs with diverse additive amount of Cr (VI). (D) The relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency F/F0 and Cr (VI) adding amount.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BSA@CDs, BSA@CDs + Cr (Ⅵ), BSA@CDs + Cr (Ⅵ), BSA@CDs + Cr (III) and BSA@CDs + Cr (Ⅵ) + HQ; (B) FI of BSA@CDs + Cr (Ⅵ) and BSA@CDs + Cr(Ⅵ) + HQ at diverse response times; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system with diverse concentration HQ (0–200 µmol/L); (D) The relationship between fluorescence recovery efficiency F/F0 and HQ adding amount (The inset is the corresponding detecting image).
(A) BSA@CDs/Cr(Ⅵ) system’s fluorescence response to different compounds; (B) BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system’s selectivity to HQ with other interfering compounds added.
Secondary structure estimation for BSA@CDs with different amount of BSA added.

head.ce:title: A “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for hydroquinone detection based on BSA doped carbon dots (BSA@CDs) from crawfish shells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By using crawfish shells as the precursor and hydrothermal synthesis, Bovine serum albumin doped carbon dots (BSA@CDs) were prepared without excessive chemical reagents. The relationship between the fluorescence properties of different BSA@CDs and BSA amount was investigated by variouscharacterization techniques. When the amount of BSA added was 30 %, the prepared BSA@CDs’ quantum yield (QY) reached 25.01 %, which was the highest. Inner Filter Effect (IFE) suggested that Cr (VI) can selectively quench the fluorescence of BSA@CDs. Cr (VI) can be reduced to Cr (III) by Hydroquinone (HQ), thus recovering the fluorescence. Accordingly, using BSA@CDs as a probe, a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor applied in HQ determination was constructed. The linear range was 10–200 µmol/L and limit of detection (LOD) was 0.18 µmol/L. Further, it has been employed to the determination of HQ in both crawfish tail meat and aquaculture water with good performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic pollutants are considered as one of the important environmental pollutants. They are significant synthetic intermediate of chemical products, widely used in industrial production, and also important by-products of oil refineries, steel mills and pulp mills [31]. Hydroquinone has a dominant influence in the manufacture of cosmetics, antioxidants, pesticides and dyes. At times, it can be unintentionally discharged into the surroundings, potentially polluting rivers and groundwater (Z. [12]. The toxicity of HQ presents a grave danger to human wellbeing, thus necessitating the development of an efficient, expeditious and dependable method of detecting it.

Currently, many analytical methods, such as chromatography, electrochemistry, have been devised to apply in HQ determination. Nevertheless, the chromatographic method has some problems such as complicated sample pretreatment steps, high detection cost, long detection time and use of toxic reagents. Furthermore, the soft and fragile electrode materials also cause many disadvantages such as short working life, limited range of detection temperature and low recovery in food samples, limiting the use of electrochemistry analysis. Compared with the methods above, fluorescence sensors have become a trend for its high sensitivity, strong selectivity, high detection speed and simple instruments.

Carbon dots (CDs) are spherical zero-dimensional C-based nanomaterial. They are commonly used in the construction of fluorescence sensors for the determination of pesticides, drugs and heavy metals due to its excellent water solubility, superb electrical conductivity, ecological friendliness, biocompatibility and unique optical properties (H. [11,14]. Hao et al. successfully prepared typical aggregation-induced emission featured SN-CDs and fabricated a multivariate probe for Cu2+, Hg2+, and BSA, with a low detection limit (Cu2+: 0.46 μmol·L−1, Hg2+: 25.8 nmol·L−1, BSA: 1.52 μmol·L−1) [8]. Traditionally, CDs are produced from non-renewable resources while biomass waste is an ideal material for preparing CDs owing to its high carbon content and wide range of sources [10]. It has been proved that the QY of CDs derived from biomass is higher than that of CDs derived from traditional chemical carbon sources [23]. This result can be attributed to that the surface defects of CDs can be rectified by N atoms, leading to the improvement of QY Liu et al. [15,17]. Also, the combination of N with other elements such as S or P can strengthen the doping effect [2,20]. It was reported that Sun et al. fabricated Fe, N co-doped carbon dots (AFC-CDs) by optimizing precursors and reaction conditions for the detection of dopamine. The LOD was 0.29 μM and 2.31 μM via fluorimetric/colorimetric mode respectively [25]. Consequently, in the study of the preparation of CDs and their derivatives, crawfish shells have been considered as suitable ingredients. They are mainly composed of chitin, calcium minerals and proteins, among which chitin has been deemed to be an outstanding nitrogenous carbon source to prepare biomass CDs. Through hydrothermal carbonization, crawfish shells’ N and S elements can also be absorbed into CDs [30]. According to this, our prior study utilized crawfish shells as green precursors to create NSCDs through hydrothermal synthesis, yielding high QY and remarkable fluorescence properties [3]. The properties of CDs are decided by both carbon-like core and organic-like surface, which mix the characteristics of carbon materials and small organic molecules, leading to great accessibility to the manipulation of structural and physicochemical properties [18,19]. However, there are few studies on the interaction between protein and other components in the preparation of biomass CDs and most of them focus on the interaction between prepared CDs and protein. Therefore, studying CDs’ fluorescence properties synthesis with chitin as the base component and different proportions of protein as the precursor is of great significance for studying the interaction of components in the process of CDs synthesis.

In this study, the influences of the amount of protein doped in CDs synthesis on the promotion of CDs’ fluorescence properties were investigated and discussed. Further, on the basis of IFE and redox reaction between Cr (VI) and HQ, a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor constructed based on BSA@CDs was established for efficient and selective detection of HQ.

Crawfish shells were acquired from seafood bazaar (Wuxi, China). The analytical reagents, i.e., Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, HCl, NaOH, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2 and other test cation were acquired from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). BSA, glucose (GLC), glycine (Gly), HQ, Phenol (PN), Catechol (CC), Resorcinol (RS) and other phenolic reagents were furnished by Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The used reagents were at least at the level of analysis.

Crawfish shells were washed and then rinsed with ultrapure water for 3 times, followed by drying for 12 h at 70℃. Then they were ground into powder and passed through an 80-mesh sieve. Then ultrasound treatment of the powder was conducted for 3 h with 0.4 mol/L HCl (1:20 w/v) added, followed by rinsing with deionized water until pH was 7. Subsequently, the powder was transferred into 1 mol/L NaOH (1:20 w/v) and reacted for 6 h at 80℃, followed by rinsing with deionized water until pH was 7.

1 G powder was taken and then mixed with different mass of BSA (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 g) in ultrapure water 25 mL, followed by a 10-min ultrasound treatment. Whereafter, the hybrid was heated for 6 h at 200℃ in an autoclave. The supernatant was filtered through a hydrophilic membrane (0.22 µM), followed by a 48-h dialysis by a cellulose membrane (1000 Da). Ultimately, the obtained BSA@CDs solution was kept at a 4℃ refrigerator for subsequent examination and utilization

A JEM-2100 apparatus (JEOL Ltd., Japan) was utilized to obtain the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of BSA@CDs. A Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Instruments Ltd., Germany) was used to get the X-ray diffraction (XRD). A Thermo Scientific™ DXR SmartRaman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) was employed to gain the Raman spectrum. A Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Ltd., U.S.A.) was utilized to acquire the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum between 4000 and 500 cm−1. An Escalab 250Xi XPS (Thermo Scientific) was applied to get the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. UV-1800 and RF-6000 spectrophotometers (Shimadzu Ltd., Japan) were used to measure the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra.

1 Mg lyophilization power of BSA@CDs was added to 1000 mL 0.2 mol/L PBS solution (pH 8.0) to form a 1 mg/L working solution. 300 μL Cr (VI) solution (80 µmol/L) was added to 2.7 mL working solution in a 5 mL centrifuge tube when detecting Cr (VI). Then 300 μL diverse concentrations of HQ solution was incorporated into the mixed solution, and then reacted at room temperature for 10 min. In the wavelength range of 380–650 nm, the fluorescence spectra were measured with the excitation wavelength being 360 nm

K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Al3+ were blended with the above BSA@CDs solution separately for examining the selectivity of Cr (VI). With a recording emission range of 380–650 nm, the fluorescence spectra were collected in the condition of excitation wavelength being 360 nm. PN, CC, RS, o-NP, m-NP, p-NP, p-MP, p-CP, p-AP, GLC, BA, Gly were blended with the working solution and determine the relative fluorescence intensity (FI) respectively for examining the selectivity of BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) to HQ. Furthermore, to explore BSA@CDs’ selectivity to Cr (VI) and BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ)’s selectivity to HQ, the effect on the determination of FI with the coexistence of Cr (VI) and the above 15 metal ions and the coexistence of HQ and the above 12 compounds was studied.

The local aquaculture water was sampled for experiment. Samples were centrifuged for 10 min (10000 rpm), followed by filtering through a hydrophilic membrane (0.22 µM). Then the solution was blended with HQ solution (0, 50, 100, and 200 µmol/L) to prepare actual samples. 2 g crawfish meat was blended with diverse HQ amount (0, 50, 100, and 200 µmol/L) overnight. A homogenizer was utilized to smash the mixture exhaustively, which was then dispersed into PBS solution (0.2 mol/L, pH 8.0). Before a 5-min ultrasonic extraction was conducted, the samples were vortex-mixed for 1 min, and then. After collecting the supernatant completely, the residue was reduplicative handled with the PSB solution. Subsequently, the supernatant was combined with the residue and blended thoroughly. At last, the sensor mentioned in 2.4 was applied to detect HQ in water samples and crawfish meat samples.

Self-dope is a significant advantage of biomass CDs, and due to its complex and diverse components, complementary enhancement among different components can be achieved during hydrothermal reaction without additional modification [18,19]. Main components of crawfish shell include chitin, protein and mineral salts. Apart from the mineral salts, which are useless for CDs synthesis, chitin and protein are of great significance in the fluorescence of CDs. In our previous study on the fluorescence QY of synthesis CDs from crawfish shells with different alkali treatment time, the QY of CDs was highly correlated with the protein residue in crawfish shell. Currently, there is a limited body of research on the interplay between proteins and other constituents during the preparation of biomass CDs, and most studies primarily are focused on the interaction between synthesized CDs and proteins (C. [21,24,28]. During the synthesis of crawfish shell CDs, chitin and protein mainly produced sugars, phenols and aldehydes in the hydrolysis process, which further form CDs through a series of reactions such as decomposition, polymerization, condensation, aromatization, nucleation and growth. However, owing to the large difference in the proportion of chitin and protein, the physicochemical properties, surface function, optical properties and fluorescence mechanism of the synthesized CDs are also different. Therefore, studying the fluorescence of CDs with chitin as the basic component and adding different proportions of proteins as precursors, which is of great significance for learning the interaction among various components in the synthesis of CDs.

In this part of the work, we used deproteinized crawfish shells as the basic carbon source to prepare BSA@CDs by adding model protein BSA instead of shrimp shell protein. We also studied the impact of different BSA additions in the physicochemical properties and fluorescence characteristics of CDs, and analyzed the correlation between their differences. Finally, we actualized the synthesis of BSA@CDs with better performance and applied to the fluorescence analysis of hydroquinone.

The morphological structure and particle size distribution of BSA@CDs can be intuitively displayed by TEM images. Fig. 1 A, B, C, D and E were TEM images of CDs prepared through hydrothermal synthesis with deproteinized crawfish shell as the main raw materials, followed by adding 0, 10 %, 20 %, 30 % and 40 % BSA respectively. The Figures showed that there were uniform distributions of CDs added 0, 20 %, 30 % and 40 % BSA, but particles aggregation phenomenon occurred in 10 %. Because this TEM image was later remade, strong Van der Waals interaction and long-time storage led to BSA@CDs stick easily and formed a certain degree of particles aggregation phenomenon. Therefore, long-term stability remains one of the challenges in the field of CDs. According to the images, the morphology of BSA@CDs was approximately circular. The average particle size was basically between 2 and 5 nm, and a few exceeded 5 nm. The HRTEM image of BSA@CDs was randomly selected to calculate the lattice spacing, and obtained the following lattice spacing data: 0.21 (0), 0.32 (10 %), 0.20 (20 %), 0.24 (30 %) and 0.24 (40 %) nm. Wherein, 0.20–0.24 nm and 0.32 nm corresponded to the (100) and (002) graphite carbon crystal planes severally (R. [16]. Different lattice spacing indicated the random growth orientation of BSA@CDs.

The crystal structure of BSA@CDs was studied through the XRD map (Fig. 2 A). All BSA@CDs present an extensive diffraction peak around 21°, which may correspond to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, manifesting its poor crystallization and amorphous property Liu et al. [15,17]. The wide diffraction peak around 42° can be divided into (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [4]. These results were consistent with those of TEM.

Fig. 2B showed the Raman spectrum of all BSA@CDs. There were two wide peaks in a range of 1347–1360 cm−1 and 1573–1587 cm−1, corresponding to the sp3 defect band (D band) and sp2 graphite band (G band) in the synthetic BSA@CDs respectively. The D band was attributed to the suspended C atom’s vibration at the end of the disordered graphite plane, while the sp2 C atom’s vibration in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice can be responsible for the formation of G band [4,32]. The two peaks’ relative intensity ratio was approximately 0.85, indicating a low degree of graphitization in BSA@CDs and there were many defective carbon structures, which may be caused by the successful doping of N atoms, S atoms and oxygen-rich functional groups in CDs. These elements can regulate the defects of CDs structure, inhibit or eliminate the original oxidation, promote radiation recombination, and lead to higher QYs [4].

Fig. 2C displayed the FT-IR spectrum. The absorption bands of CDs containing BSA are significantly different from those without BSA. These results suggested that chitin and proteins, as important components of biomass, directly affected the functional properties of CDs. Chitin contributed to the formation of the carbon skeleton, while proteins had a vital impact on the functions in C-based materials [7]. The tensile vibration of –OH in BSA@CDs may be related to the wideband at 3400 cm−1. The surface polar functional groups made the CDs have high hydrophilicity and good dispersibility. The results demonstrated that when freeze-dried CDs were exposed to air, CDs without BSA absorbed moisture faster, while CDs with BSA maintained loose powdery forms, indicating a certain level of hydrophobicity in BSA@CDs. This can be attributed to the fact that hydrophobic interactions served as the primary driving force for protein folding, and the hydrophobic residues present in BSA-synthesized CDs play a crucial role in conferring hydrophobic properties to BSA@CDs. In addition to the hydrophobic residues, hydrophilic groups such as –OH and –COOH were almost unobservable in BSA@CDs, thus having a dominant influence in the hydrophobicity of BSA@CDs. N–H bond’s stretching vibration can explain the absorption peak observed at approximately 3200 cm−1. Meanwhile, the wide absorption band which ranged from 3500 to 3100 cm−1 indicated an interconnectedness between the stretching vibrations of both N–H and –OH functional groups in BSA@CDs through hydrogen bonding [4]. The amide I band (1656 cm−1) and amide II band (1573 cm−1) represented the prominent infrared spectral features of proteins. In the infrared spectrum of BSA@CDs, there was a gradual enhancement in the intensities of both amide I and amide II bands, which can be attributed to the increase in BSA addition [33]. The findings showed that the protein's structure underwent damage, while the processes of dehydration and carbonization remained incomplete. Additionally, the peak observed at 1656 cm−1 may corresponded to the absorption peak associated with stretching vibrations of –COOH [20]. C-N’s vibration can be responsible for the characteristic absorption band at 1400 cm−1, and further illustrated the successful doping of N atoms in BSA@CDs [20,26]. Furthermore, absorption peaks at 2964, 1454 and 1110 cm−1 can be classified as C–H telescopic vibration, C–H deformation vibration and C-O telescopic vibration severally, while the band near 1301 cm−1 belonged to C=S, and the energy band strength enhanced with the increase of BSA addition [13,32]. Therefore, the introduction of S atoms in BSA@CDs was related to the addition of BSA. Additionally, when C=S was connected to one or two N atoms, it would vibrate with no localization, which may produce a strong vibration coupling effect. Due to the above effect, these nitrogen-containing derivatives exhibited three absorption peaks in ranges of 1395–1570 cm−1, 1260–1420 cm−1, and 940–1140 cm−1, which can be labeled as I, II, and III bands of −N-C=S respectively (C. N. R. [22]. The above FT-IR results confirmed that the hydrothermal synthesis method had successfully introduced oxygen-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-rich functional groups from BSA into BSA@CDs. Additionally, secondary structure of BSA@CDs was estimated by the results of FT-IR. As can be seen from Table 1 , CDs contained 52.01 % α-helix, 1.63 % β-sheet, 9.87 % turns and 36.49 % random coils, respectively. With the addition of BSA, the percentage of α-helix decreased and the percentage of β-sheet increased, indicating that CDs effectively bounded to the protein.

As depicted in Fig. 3 , the chemical composition and elemental state of BSA@CDs were tested using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full-scan XPS spectra of BSA@CDs (Fig. 3A) revealed the presence of carbon (C1s, 284.8 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.8 eV), oxygen (O1s, 531.8 eV), and sulfur (S2p, 163.8 eV) in all CDs with BSA, while only carbon (C1s, 284.8 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.8 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 531.8 eV) were detected in CDs with no BSA. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the nitrogen in CDs without BSA, was measured to be 6.04 %, indicating the successful doping of abundant nitrogen from chitin through hydrothermal carbonization. When BSA was added, the content of nitrogen had increased significantly, up to 13.72 %. Furthermore, sulfur elements were observed within the CD structure with a content of approximately 0.7 %. The findings confirmed the crucial role played by BSA in facilitating the synthesis reaction of CDs and effectively introducing N and S atoms into the structure of BSA@CDs.

C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) exhibited four distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.1 eV, and 287.9 eV, which can correspond to the bonds of C–C, C-N, C-O, and C=O severally. Conversely, CDs with BSA displayed three prominent peaks at 284.8 eV, with ranges of 286.1–286.3 eV and 287.6–287.9 eV which can be interpreted as the bonds of C–C, C-N and C=O severally [29]. Moreover, the N1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3C) exhibited four distinct peaks which can correspond to pyridine N (398.5 eV), pyrrole N (399.5 eV), amine N (400.3 eV), and graphite N (401.5 eV) [9]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) was deconvoluted into three regions in ranges of 530.6–531.1 eV, 531.2–531.8 eV, and 532.3–532.8 eV, which were caused by C=O, C-OH, and C-O-C functionalities in sequence [6]. In the CDs’ S2p spectrum (Fig. 3E), two distinct sulfur peaks were observed in CDs with BSA in ranges of 363.1––363.8 eV and 167.9–168.2 eV, corresponding to C-S and C-SO3 bonds in sequence [1]. FT-IR and XPS spectrum came to a conclusion that BSA@CDs were composed of functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur. Therefore, CDs manifested significant water-solubility, excellent dispersion and ideal optical properties.

The supernatant after the synthesis of BSA@CDs was displayed in Fig. 4 A. Under the same experimental conditions, the color of the supernatant can reflect the degree of carbonization and the concentration of different BSA@CDs. Fig. 4B showed the photos of the supernatant lighted by ultraviolet at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All the supernatant emitted blue fluorescence and showed obvious IFE. When the supernatant was diluted to 100 times (Fig. 4C and D), IFE was significantly dropped while fluorescence effect was strongly enhanced. Fig. 4E and F depicted respectively that fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption were performed to examine CDs’ optical properties. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was about 440 nm. FI was significantly stronger with the amount of BSA increasing. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields were 10.13 %, 12.63 %, 19.74 %, 25.01 % and 23.41 % respectively and reached the maximum when the amount of BSA was 30 %, suggesting the conclusion above. XPS analysis showed that the change of fluorescence effect was caused by the co-doped with N and S in the structure of BSA@CDs. The co-doped with N and S generated superb fluorescence effect and adjusted the surface defects which can eliminate or inhibit the original oxidation state, promote radiation recombination and lead to higher QY Liu et al. [15,17]. In addition, the heteroatom doped in the carbon structure of BSA@CDs can also adjust initial band gap and form new energy level. Therefore, using crawfish shells as precursor to synthesize CDs can significantly improve the fluorescence property of CDs by using BSA as ligand without any modification

Fig. 4F depicted BSA@CDs’ UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorophores, which distributed on the surface of CDs, contributed to the characteristic peaks [27]. BSA@CDs had two characteristic peaks at 275 nm and 330 nm, while CDs without BSA added had only one absorption peak at 281 nm. In addition, the molecular fluorophores containing amide are the precondition for the formation of C nucleus and eventual effect on the fluorescence of the system. The peaks appearing at < 300 nm were caused by π-π* transition with conjugated C=C corresponding to C nucleus [26]. However, another study suggested that the peaks owing to n-π* transition at 322 nm and 328 nm may correspond to non-bonding orbital effect of pyridine N and pyrrole N [18,19]. The intensity of the first absorption peak (275 nm) of BSA@CDs was significantly higher than that of CS-CDs, possibly due to the effect of pyridine N and graphite N [18,19]. The intensity of the second absorption peak (330 nm) of BSA@CDs was strongly higher than that of CS-CDs, which may be related to amine N and pyrrole N [18,19]. The results suggested that self-passivation and self-doping of the compound can strengthen FI during the synthesis of BSA@CDs. In addition, the fluorescence attenuation spectra of BSA@CDs in the absence and presence of Cr (Ⅵ) were recorded, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was further explained. As shown in Fig. 4G, the average fluorescence lifetime of BSA@CDs and BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) was 8.39 ns and 8.05 ns, respectively. After the addition of Cr (Ⅵ), the fluorescence lifetime changed little, indicating that there was no obvious electron transfer process between BSA@CDs and Cr (Ⅵ) [5]. This phenomenon ruled out the occurrence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and suggested that IFE was the main quenching mechanism.

Based on the results of the characterization analysis above, BSA@CDs with 30 % BSA added which has the best fluorescence property was used as the probe for subsequent experiments. For the sake of eliminating probable interference in subsequent actual sample determination and enhance the capacity of BSA@CDs to HQ detection, various factors were optimized. Fluorescence response intensity ratio (F0/F) was utilized to evaluate fluorescence quenching effect of Cr (VI), in which F and F0 represented FI of BSA@CDs with Cr (VI) added or not respectively. Fig. 5 A showed the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the system gradually increased when the added BSA@CDs ranged from 20 to 80 µg mL−1 and reached the maximum when the BSA@CDs concentration was 80 µg mL−1. The influence of pH in the fluorescence response of BSA@CDs was displayed in Fig. 5B. In the range of pH 5–8, the fluorescence quenching efficiency gradually increased and reached the maximum when pH was 8. Fig. 5C displayed that after Cr (VI) was blended into the system, fluorescence quenching efficiency increased swiftly and a steady fluorescence response intensity was obtained in less than 1 min, indicating that the reaction time was fast. Therefore, the optimized detection conditions were as follows: pH was 8, reaction time was 1 min and BSA@CDs additive amount was 80 µg mL−1. Under these conditions above, linear response range and sensitivity of BSA@CDs to Cr (VI) were investigated by the addition of various Cr (VI) amount (0–80 µmol/L). BSA@CDs’ FI reduced with the augmentation of Cr (VI) concentration by degrees, shown in Fig. 6 C. Fig. 6D demonstrated the relationship between F/F0 and the amount of Cr (VI) added. In the range of 1–30 µmol/L, CDs had a good linear relationship. The equation was F/F0 = -0.0211[C] + 1.0146 (R2 = 0.9957), where C represented the concentration of Cr (VI). CDs’ fluorescence was obviously quenched by Cr (VI) system. Fluorescence quenching rate reached the maximum (88 %) when the concentration of Cr (VI) was 80 µmol/L. Therefore, in the subsequent fluorescence opening experiments, Cr (VI) adding amount was 80 µmol/L.

Due to the strong oxidation of Cr (Ⅵ) and the reducibility of HQ, a strong redox reaction can occur between Cr (Ⅵ) and HQ. In this redox reaction, HQ is oxidized to p-benzoquinone and Cr (Ⅵ) is reduced to Cr (III). Fig. 7 A elucidated that HQ and Cr (III) had no influence in the BSA@CDs’ fluorescence, while Cr (Ⅵ) can quench BSA@CDs’ fluorescence. In addition, when HQ was blended into BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system, fluorescence can be partially recovered. This phenomenon may be illustrated that HQ stably reduced Cr (Ⅵ) to Cr (III), thus eliminating IFE effectively and recovering BSA@CDs fluorescence. Consequently, BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system had the capacity to be used as an “turn-on” fluorescence probe for the determination of HQ.

The quenching kinetics of HQ on BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system’s fluorescence was also studied, thus obtaining the best HQ detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 7B, after 80 µmol/L Cr (Ⅵ) was added, the FI decreased promptly and remained stable in 60 min. After adding 100 µmol/L HQ, the FI augmented quickly and remained stable after 10 min. Accordingly, the optimal recovery time was 10 min. BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) can be used as a good sensor system for HQ detection due to rapid response and good stability.

Different concentrations of HQ (0–200 µmol/L) were blended into BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system for analyzing sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor. Fig. 7C displayed that FI recovered with the rise adding amount of HQ little by little. Fig. 7D manifested that in the range of 10–200 µmol/L, there was a good linear relationship between F/F0 and HQ adding amount in the range of 10–200 µmol/L. The equation was F/F0 = 0.0089[C] + 1.0192 (R2 = 0.9956), where C represented HQ concentration. LOD of HQ was 0.18 µmol/L. The inset is the corresponding detecting image, showing the relationship between fluorescence intensity and HQ adding amount. These results figured that the BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system can be expected as a fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for HQ detection.

The selectivity of BSA@CDs to Cr (VI) was tested by different ions. 80 µmol/L Cr (VI) or other ions were blended with the working solution, followed by determining the relative FI. Fig. 6A showed that Cr (VI) had the strongest effect of fluorescence quenching on BSA@CDs, while other metal ions’ fluorescence quenching effect was weak. Therefore, the effect of these metal ions was almost negligible. Additionally, to determine BSA@CDs’ selectivity to Cr (VI), 30 µmol/L of Cr (VI) was incorporated into BSA@CDs solution in which 100 µmol/L of other ions existed (Fig. 6B). BSA@CDs’ fluorescence response to Cr (VI) did not change after the addition of higher concentration of other metal ions, indicating that other cations had little influence in BSA@CDs’ fluorescence. Hence, BSA@CDs has high selectivity for Cr (VI).

The selectivity of BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system was investigated by different compounds. 100 µmol/L HQ or other interfering compounds were incorporated into the working solution, followed by determining the relative FI. Fig. 8 A displayed that HQ had the strongest effect of fluorescence recovering on BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system, while other compounds had no effect on fluorescence recovering. In addition, to determine BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system’s selectivity to HQ, 100 µmol/L of HQ was added to BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system solution in which 200 µmol/L of other interfering compounds existed (Fig. 8B). BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system’s fluorescence response to HQ did not change with the existence of higher concentration of other interfering compounds, indicating that other interfering compounds had little influence in BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system’s fluorescence. Consequently, BSA@CDs/Cr (VI) system had high selectivity for HQ. In addition, the cost of the established method was calculated to be no more than 1 dollar, indicating its potential for practical use.

BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system was employed to analyze HQ in aquaculture water and crawfish tail meat. Considering that HQ was not determined in these actual samples, the accuracy and applicability of the fluorescence sensor was evaluated by the spiked recovery test with diverse additive amount of HQ (0, 50, 100 and 200 µmol/L) in actual samples. Table 2 displayed that the recoveries ranged from 96.52 % to 105.37 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) < 6 %. The results improved the reliability and feasibility of HQ detection in actual samples by BSA@CDs/Cr (Ⅵ) system.

BSA@CDs were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis using crawfish shell as a sustainable precursor. Notably, when the BSA addition was 30 %, the QY of BSA@CDs was 25.01 %, which reached the maximum. The optical properties of BSA@CDs were closely related to their physicochemical properties, where the surface doped with N and S and their synergistic effects played a decisive role. Owing to IFE, the fluorescence of BSA@CDs is able to be selectively quenched by Cr (VI), which can be reduced to Cr (III) by HQ to recover fluorescence. Accordingly, a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for determination of HQ was designed using BSA@CDs as a probe. with a linear range of 10 to 200 µmol/L and a LOD of 0.18 µmol/L. Moreover, the sensor has been successfully used for HQ analysis in fresh crawfish tail meat and aquacultural water, which exhibited good recoveries (96.52–105.37 %).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported in this article was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Changzhou (Project No. CE20232005), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province, China.
